rounds.blurb: V-J Day in Times Square: a sailor's kiss as news of Japan's surrender breaks.
rounds.credit: Victor Jorgensen, U.S. Navy — public domain
rounds.story: When Japan's surrender was announced, Times Square erupted. Navy photographer Victor Jorgensen caught the same kiss as Eisenstaedt's famous LIFE frame from a different angle — and the government version is the one that belongs to everyone.

rounds.blurb: Times Square, deserted at midday in the first spring of the pandemic.
rounds.story: New York was then the pandemic's global epicenter, losing hundreds of people a day while the Crossroads of the World stood empty at noon. The billboards kept playing to nobody.

rounds.blurb: The Space Needle rises over the Century 21 Exposition — the Seattle World's Fair.
rounds.story: Built in about 400 days for the fair, the Space Needle anchored an exposition themed on life in the year 2000 — ten million visitors rode the new monorail and previewed touch-tone phones. Elvis filmed a movie on the grounds that fall.

rounds.blurb: Falcon Heavy's first flight leaves Pad 39A — with a Roadster for a payload.
rounds.story: The demo flight carried a Tesla Roadster with a spacesuited mannequin at the wheel, now orbiting the Sun beyond Mars. Both side boosters landed back at the Cape in formation — the moment reusable rockets stopped looking experimental.

rounds.blurb: Babe Ruth in his first season as a Yankee — the year the home run changed baseball.
rounds.story: The Yankees had just bought Ruth from Boston for $125,000; he answered with 54 home runs — more than any other entire team in the American League managed that season. The dead-ball era was over, and Boston's ensuing 86-year drought became the Curse of the Bambino.
